hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'neminis' or 'nullius' — 'nemo' is nominative

hints.to eat: 'Esse/edere' (from 'edo') means to eat — this 'est' is from 'sum' (to be)

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'fuit' — 'est' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'erit' — 'est' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'est' is indicative

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat) — confusingly shares the infinitive 'esse'
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)

steps.prompt: What type of relative clause does 'qui reliquerit' form?

hints.simple relative clause (indicative): 'Reliquerit' is subjunctive, not indicative — after 'nemo' this is a clause of characteristic
hints.purpose relative clause: Purpose relatives express intention — this characterizes the type of person who does not exist
hints.causal relative clause: Causal relatives explain why — this describes a characteristic kind of person

hints.to seek: 'To seek' is 'requirere' — 'reliquerit' comes from 'relinquere' (to leave behind)
hints.to release: 'To release' is 'relaxare' — 'relinquere' means to leave behind

steps.reveal: reliquerit = has left / who may have left

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect indicative would be 'reliquit' — 'reliquerit' with -erit is subjunctive
hints.future perfect indicative: Future perfect and perfect subjunctive look identical — context (relative clause of characteristic) determines subjunctive
hints.pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'reliquisset' — 'reliquerit' is perfect subjunctive

hints.linquo, linquere, liqui, lictum: These are the parts of 'linquere' (to leave) — the base verb without the re- prefix
hints.religo, religare, religavi, religatum: These are the parts of 'religare' (to bind back) — different verb
hints.requiro, requirere, requisivi, requisitu: These are the parts of 'requirere' (to seek again) — different compound

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'domus' — 'domum' with -um is accusative

steps.reveal: propter regnum Dei = for the sake of the kingdom of God

hints.both are nominative: 'Propter' requires the accusative — 'regnum' is accusative; 'Dei' is genitive of possession
hints.both are genitive: 'Regnum' cannot be genitive (it would be 'regni') — it is accusative with 'propter'
hints.'regnum' is ablative, 'Dei' is genitive: 'Propter' takes the accusative, not ablative — 'regnum' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'regni' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'regni' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'regni' ends in -i

translation: Truly I say to you, there is no one who has left home for the sake of the kingdom of God
